raw_question_text: Consider a $2-\text{cm}$ thick plastic scintillator directly coupled to the surface of a photomultiplier with a gain of $10^{6}$. A $10-\text{GeV}$ particle beam is incident on the scintillator as shown in Fig. 4.8(a). Suppose one could detect a signal on the anode of as little as $10^{-12}$ coulomb. If the beam particle is a neutron, estimate what is the smallest laboratory angle that it could scatter elastically from a proton in the scintillator and still be detected?
normalized_question_text: Consider a $2-\text{cm}$ thick plastic scintillator directly coupled to the surface of a photomultiplier with a gain of $10^{6}$. A $10-\text{GeV}$ particle beam is incident on the scintillator as shown in Fig. 4.8(a). Suppose one could detect a signal on the anode of as little as $10^{-12}$ coulomb. If the beam particle is a neutron, estimate what is the smallest laboratory angle that it could scatter elastically from a proton in the scintillator and still be detected?
reasoning_chain:
Figure $4.8(\text{b})$ shows a neutron scatters by a small angle $\theta$ in the laboratory frame. Its momentum is changed by an amount $p \theta$ normal to the direction of motion. This is the momentum of the recoiling nucleus. Then the kinetic energy acquired by it is
$$
\frac{p^{2} \theta^{2}}{2 m}
$$
where $m$ is the mass of the recoiling nucleus. As an energy loss of 4 MeV corresponds to $3.2 \times 10^{-10}\text{C}$ of anode charge, the detection threshold of $10^{-12}\text{C}$ implies that recoil energy as little as 12.5 keV can be detected. Hence the smallest laboratory scattering angle $\theta_{\text{min}}$ that can be detected is given by
$$
\theta_{\text{min}}^{2}=\frac{2 m_{p}}{p_{n}^{2}} \times 12.5 \times 10^{3}=\frac{2 \times 10^{9}}{\left(10^{10}\right)^{2}} \times 12.5 \times 10^{3}=2.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{rad}^{2}
$$
i.e.
$$
\theta_{\text{min}}=5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{rad}
$$
assuming the recoiling nucleus is a proton.

raw_question_text: Suppose you have been supplied with a number of sheets of two types of optically active material. Sheets of type $P$ are perfect polarizers: they transmit (normally incident) light polarized parallel to some axis $\boldsymbol{n}$ and absorb light polarized perpendicular to $\boldsymbol{n}$. Sheets of type $Q$ are quarter-wave plates: they advance the phase of (normally incident) light polarized parallel to some axis $\boldsymbol{m}$ by $\frac{\pi}{2}$ relative to light polarized perpendicular to $\boldsymbol{m}$. Describe how to combine these materials to produce a device which converts incident light polarized parallel to some axis $\boldsymbol{x}$ to outgoing light polarized perpendicular to $\boldsymbol{x}$ (see Fig.~2.71). What is the loss in intensity in the device you have constructed?
normalized_question_text: Suppose you have been supplied with a number of sheets of two types of optically active material. Sheets of type $P$ are perfect polarizers: they transmit (normally incident) light polarized parallel to some axis $\boldsymbol{n}$ and absorb light polarized perpendicular to $\boldsymbol{n}$. Sheets of type $Q$ are quarter-wave plates: they advance the phase of (normally incident) light polarized parallel to some axis $\boldsymbol{m}$ by $\frac{\pi}{2}$ relative to light polarized perpendicular to $\boldsymbol{m}$. Describe how to combine these materials to produce a device which converts incident light polarized parallel to some axis $\boldsymbol{x}$ to outgoing light polarized perpendicular to $\boldsymbol{x}$ (see Fig.~2.71). What is the loss in intensity in the device you have constructed?
reasoning_chain: Place two P-type sheets together such that the first sheet has its $\mathbf{n}$ axis at $45^\circ$ to the $\mathbf{x}$ axis and the second has its $\mathbf{n}$ axis at $90^\circ$ to the $\mathbf{x}$ axis. The combined sheet will convert an incident light polarized parallel to $\mathbf{x}$ into an outgoing light polarized perpendicular to $\mathbf{x}$. The outgoing intensity is reduced to $I_{0} \cos^4 45^\circ = I_{0}/4$, where $I_{0}$ is the incident intensity, according to the law of Malus.

raw_question_text: The pions that are produced when protons strike the target at Fermilab are not all moving parallel to the initial proton beam. A focusing device, called a "horn", (actually two of them are used as a pair) is used to deflect the pions so as to cause them to move more closely towards the proton beam direction. This device consists of an inner cylindrical conductor along which a current flows in one direction and an outer cylindrical conductor along which the current returns. Between these two surfaces there is produced a toroidal magnetic field that deflects the mesons that pass through this region. By what angle would a $100\,\mathrm{GeV}/c$ meson be deflected if it traversed 2 meters of one horn's magnetic field at very nearly this radius of 15 cm ?
normalized_question_text: The pions that are produced when protons strike the target at Fermilab are not all moving parallel to the initial proton beam. A focusing device, called a "horn" (actually two of them are used as a pair), is used to deflect the pions so as to cause them to move more closely towards the proton beam direction. This device consists of an inner cylindrical conductor along which a current flows in one direction and an outer cylindrical conductor along which the current returns. Between these two surfaces there is produced a toroidal magnetic field that deflects the mesons that pass through this region. By what angle would a $100\,\mathrm{GeV}/c$ meson be deflected if it traversed 2 meters of one horn's magnetic field at very nearly this radius of 15 cm?
reasoning_chain: For a $100\,\mathrm{GeV}/c$ meson, noting that $p=\frac{m_{0}v}{\sqrt{1-\frac{v^{2}}{c^{2}}}}=100\,\mathrm{GeV}/c$ so that $v\approx c$, the deflecting force is $F=eBv=1.6\times10^{-19}\times0.41\times3\times10^{8}=2.0\times10^{-11}\,\mathrm{N}$. The deflected transverse distance is $x=\frac{1}{2}\frac{F}{m}\left(\frac{1}{c}\right)^2=0.0025\,\mathrm{m}$, and hence the angle of deflection is $\theta=\arctan\left(\frac{0.0025}{2}\right)=0.0013\,\mathrm{rad}$.

For $T_{2}$, $\beta=\frac{I_{c}}{I_{b}}=\frac{1400}{100}=14$ so that $T_{2}$ is saturated. Thus $V_{\mathrm{C}}=5\,\mathrm{V},\; V_{\mathrm{D}}=5-0.7=4.3\,\mathrm{V},\; V_{\mathrm{E}}=V_{\mathrm{D}}-0.7=3.6\,\mathrm{V}$.

raw_question_text: A thin spherical shell of radius $R$ has a fixed charge $+q$ distributed uniformly over its surface. A small circular section (radius $r \ll R$) of charge is removed from the surface. The cut section is replaced and the sphere is set in motion rotating with constant angular velocity $\omega=\omega_{0}$ about the $z$-axis. Now the sphere's angular velocity increases linearly with time: \(\omega=\omega_{0}+kt\). Calculate the line integral of the electric field around the circular path $P$ (located at the center of the sphere with normal along the $+z$ axis and radius $r_{P} \ll R$).
normalized_question_text: A thin spherical shell of radius $R$ has a fixed charge $+q$ distributed uniformly over its surface. A small circular section (radius $r \ll R$) of charge is removed from the surface. The cut section is replaced and the sphere is set in motion rotating with constant angular velocity $\omega=\omega_{0}$ about the $z$-axis. Now the sphere's angular velocity increases linearly with time: \(\omega=\omega_{0}+kt\). Calculate the line integral of the electric field around the circular path $P$ (located at the center of the sphere with normal along the $+z$ axis and radius $r_{P} \ll R$).
reasoning_chain: Consider a ring of width $R\,d\theta$ as shown in Fig. 2.25. The surface current density on the ring is $\frac{\theta}{4 \pi R^{2}} \cdot \omega R \sin\theta$. The contributions of a pair of symmetrical points on the ring to the magnetic field at the center of the sphere will sum up to a resultant in the $z$-direction. Thus the total contribution of the ring to the magnetic field is given by the Biot-Savart law as $$dB_{z}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}\int_{\varphi=0}^{2\pi}\frac{q}{4\pi R^{2}}\omega R \sin\theta\cdot\frac{R}{R^{3}}\sin\theta R\,d\theta\cdot R\sin\theta\,d\varphi=\frac{\mu_{0}q\omega}{8\pi R}\sin^{3}\theta\,d\theta,$$ Hence $$B_{z}=\frac{\mu_{0}q\omega}{8\pi R}\int_{0}^{\pi}\sin^{3}\theta\,d\theta=\frac{\mu_{0}q\omega}{6\pi R}.$$ As $r_{P} \ll R$, the magnetic induction can be taken to be uniform in the circular loop $P$. The magnetic flux crossing $P$ is then $$\phi=\pi r_{P}^{2}B=\frac{\mu_{0}r_{P}^{2}\omega q}{6R}=\frac{\mu_{0}q r_{P}^{2}(\omega_{0}+kt)}{6R}.$$ Hence $$\oint_{P}\mathbf{E}\cdot d\mathbf{l}=-\frac{d\phi}{dt}=\frac{\mu_{0}kr_{P}^{2}q}{6R}.$$

raw_question_text: A circular aperture of radius $a$ is uniformly illuminated by plane waves of wavelength $\lambda$. Let the $\boldsymbol{z}$-axis coincide with the aperture axis, with $z=0$ at the aperture, and with the incident flux travelling from negative values of $z$ toward $z=0$ (Fig.~2.30). Find the values of $z$ at which the intensity of illumination on the axis is zero (Fresnel diffraction). You may assume $z \gg a$.
normalized_question_text: A circular aperture of radius $a$ is uniformly illuminated by plane waves of wavelength $\lambda$. Let the $\boldsymbol{z}$-axis coincide with the aperture axis, with $z=0$ at the aperture, and with the incident flux travelling from negative values of $z$ toward $z=0$ (Fig.~2.30). Find the values of $z$ at which the intensity of illumination on the axis is zero (Fresnel diffraction). You may assume $z \gg a$.
reasoning_chain:
Fresnel's half-period zone construction shows that if the number of half-period zones is even for a point on the axis, the intensity of illumination at this point will be zero. As
$$
N = \frac{a^{2}}{\lambda z}
$$
or equivalently
$$
z = \frac{a^{2}}{\lambda N}
$$
the intensity at the point $z$ will be zero for $N = 2, 4, 6, \ldots$

raw_question_text: The Stark effect. The energy levels of the $n=2$ states of atomic hydrogen are illustrated in Fig. 5.14. The $S_{1/2}$ and $P_{1/2}$ levels are degenerate at an energy $\varepsilon_{0}$ and the $P_{3/2}$ level is degenerate at an energy $\varepsilon_{0}+A$. A uniform static electric field $E$ applied to the atom shifts the states to energies $\varepsilon_{1}$, $\varepsilon_{2}$ and $\varepsilon_{3}$. Assuming that all states other than these three are far enough away to be neglected, determine the energy of the $P_{3/2}$ level to second order in the electric field $E$, using the given perturbation Hamiltonian matrix elements.
normalized_question_text: The Stark effect. The energy levels of the $n=2$ states of atomic hydrogen are illustrated in Fig. 5.14. The $S_{1/2}$ and $P_{1/2}$ levels are degenerate at an energy $\varepsilon_{0}$ and the $P_{3/2}$ level is degenerate at an energy $\varepsilon_{0}+A$. A uniform static electric field $E$ applied to the atom shifts the states to energies $\varepsilon_{1}$, $\varepsilon_{2}$, and $\varepsilon_{3}$. Assuming that all states other than these three are far enough away to be neglected, determine the energy of the $P_{3/2}$ level to second order in the electric field $E$, using the given perturbation Hamiltonian matrix elements.
reasoning_chain:
Suppose the matrix elements of the perturbation Hamiltonian \(H'=-e\,\mathbf{E}\cdot\mathbf{r}\) are
 egin{tabular}{cccc}
 & \(P_{3/2}\) & \(P_{1/2}\) & \(S_{1/2}\) \
\(P_{3/2}\) & 0 & 0 & \(b\) \
\(P_{1/2}\) & 0 & 0 & \(a\) \
\(S_{1/2}\) & \(b^{*}\) & \(a^{*}\) & 0
\end{tabular},
since \(\langle l'|H'|langle=0\) for states of the same parity (Problem 5037). Then for energy level \(P_{3/2}\), we have
\[
 egin{aligned}
E_{P3/2} &= E_{P3/2}^{(0)}+ rac{|\langle P_{3/2}|H'|S_{1/2}angle|^{2}}{E_{P3/2}^{(0)}-E_{S1/2}^{(0)} } \
&= \varepsilon_{0}+\Delta+ rac{|b|^{2}}{A}.
\end{aligned}
\]

raw_question_text: A flyball governor consists of two masses $m$ connected to arms of length $l$ and a mass $M$ as shown in Fig. 2.68. The assembly is constrained to rotate around a shaft on which the mass $M$ can slide up and down without friction. Neglect the mass of the arms, air friction, and assume that the diameter of the mass $M$ is small. Suppose the shaft is now allowed to rotate freely. Does the frequency of small oscillation change? If so, calculate the new value.
normalized_question_text: A flyball governor consists of two masses $m$ connected to arms of length $l$ and a mass $M$ as shown in Fig. 2.68. The assembly is constrained to rotate around a shaft on which the mass $M$ can slide up and down without friction. Neglect the mass of the arms, air friction, and assume that the diameter of the mass $M$ is small. Suppose the shaft is now allowed to rotate freely. Does the frequency of small oscillation change? If so, calculate the new value.
reasoning_chain: When the shaft is free to rotate, using the Lagrangian with $\omega=\dot{\varphi}$ leads to the conserved quantity $\dot{\varphi}\sin^{2}\theta=c$. The subsequent Lagrange equations yield, at equilibrium ($\ddot{\theta}=0$, $\dot{\theta}=0$, $\theta=\theta_{0}$), the condition $$mc^{2}\frac{\cos\theta_{0}}{\sin^{3}\theta_{0}}=(m+M)g\sin\theta_{0}.$$ For small oscillations about $\theta_{0}$ with $\theta=\theta_{0}+\theta'$ and appropriate linearizations, the equation of motion becomes $$ (m+2M\sin^{2}\theta_{0})l\ddot{\theta}'+(m+M)g\frac{(1+3\cos^{2}\theta_{0})}{\cos\theta_{0}}\theta'=0. $$ Hence, the oscillation frequency is $$f=\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{(m+M)g(1+3\cos^{2}\theta_{0})}{(m+2M\sin^{2}\theta_{0})l\cos\theta_{0}}}.$$

raw_question_text: For the combination of one prism and 2 lenses shown (Fig. 1.42), find the location and size of the final image when the object, length 1 cm, is located as shown in the figure.
normalized_question_text: For the combination of one prism and 2 lenses shown (Fig. 1.42), find the location and size of the final image when the object, length 1 cm, is located as shown in the figure.
raw_answer_text: $10 \mathrm{cm}$ to the left of the second lens; $0.5 \mathrm{cm}$
normalized_answer_text: $10 \mathrm{cm}$ to the left of the second lens; $0.5 \mathrm{cm}$
reasoning_chain: For the right-angle prism, $n=1.5$, the critical angle $\alpha=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{n}\right)=$ $42^{\circ}$, which is smaller than the angle of incidence, $45^{\circ}$, at the hypotenuse of the prism. Therefore total internal reflection occurs, which forms a virtual image. The prism, equivalent to a glass plate of thickness 6 cm, would cause an image shift of $$\Delta L=6\left(1-\frac{1}{n}\right)=2 \mathrm{~cm}$$ Thus the effective object distance for the first lens is $$\nu_{1}=10+6+(6-2)=20 \mathrm{~cm}$$ As $u_{1}=f_{1}$, we have $v_{1}=\infty$. Then for the second lens, $u_{2}=\infty$. With $f_{2}=-10 \mathrm{~cm}$, we get $$v_{2}=f_{2}=-10 \mathrm{~cm}$$ Hence the final image is inverted and virtual, 10 cm to the left of the second lens. The size of the image is $$l_{2}=\left|\frac{f_{2}}{f_{1}}\right| \cdot 1=0.5 \mathrm{~cm}$$

raw_question_text: Looking through a small hole is a well-known method to improve sight. If your eyes are near-sighted and can focus an object 20 cm away without using any glasses, estimate the required diameter of the hole through which you would have good sight for objects far away.
normalized_question_text: Looking through a small hole is a well-known method to improve sight. If your eyes are near-sighted and can focus an object 20 cm away without using any glasses, estimate the required diameter of the hole through which you would have good sight for objects far away.
reasoning_chain: Inside the human eye the distance between the crystalline lens and the retina is 20 mm . Using the lens formula $\frac{1}{u}+\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}$, we get $f=19 \mathrm{~cm}$ for the image distance 2.0 cm and the object 20 cm away. As Fig. 1.51 shows, a lens focuses an object infinitely far away at the focal point F and forms a disk AB on the retina 1.0 cm behind F . An opening placed in front of the eye lens determines the diameter of the beam of light entering the eye. From the geometry, $d=\overline{\mathrm{CD}}=19 \overline{\mathrm{AB}}$. On account of the resolving angular limitation of the eye, which is about $1^{\prime}=3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{rad}$, we get a transverse resolving limitation of $\overline{\mathrm{AB}}_{\mathrm{m}}=3 \times 10^{-4} \times 20=6 \times 10^{-3}$ mm . If the diameter of the opening $d<19 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}=0.12 \mathrm{~mm}$, we would have good sight for objects far away.

raw_question_text: In Fig. 2.71, the cylindrical cavity is symmetric about its long axis. For the purposes of this problem, it can be approximated as a coaxial cable (which has inductance and capacitance) shorted at one end and connected to a parallel plate disk capacitor at the other. Derive an expression for the lowest resonant frequency of the cavity. Neglect end and edge effects ( $h \gg r_{2}, d \ll r_{1}$ ).
normalized_question_text: In Fig. 2.71, the cylindrical cavity is symmetric about its long axis. For the purposes of this problem, it can be approximated as a coaxial cable (which has inductance and capacitance) shorted at one end and connected to a parallel plate disk capacitor at the other. Derive an expression for the lowest resonant frequency of the cavity. Neglect end and edge effects ($h \gg r_{2}, d \ll r_{1}$).
reasoning_chain: To find the inductance and capacitance per unit length of the coaxial cable, we suppose that the inside and outside conductors respectively carry currents $I$ and $-I$ and uniform linear charges $\lambda$ and $-\lambda$. Use cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$ with the $z$-axis along the axis of the cable. Let the direction of flow of the current in the inner conductor be along the $+z$ direction. From Problem 2022 the inductance and capacitance per unit length of the coaxial cable are $L=\frac{\mu_{0} h}{2 \pi} \ln \frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}$, $C=\frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} h}{\ln \frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}}$. The capacitance of the parallel-plate condenser connected to the coaxial cable is $C_{0}=\frac{\pi \varepsilon_{0} r_{1}^{2}}{d}$. Hence the lowest resonant angular frequency of the cavity is $\omega_{0}=\frac{1}{L\left(C+C_{0}\right)}=\frac{2 d c^{2}}{h\left(2 d h+r_{1}^{2}\ln \frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)}$.

raw_question_text: A sound field is created by an arrangement of identical line sources grouped into two identical arrays of $N$ sources each as shown (Fig. 2.62) below.\nAll of the radiators lie in a plane perpendicular to the page, and produce waves of wavelength $\theta, N, c$ and $d$, the distance between the centers of the arrays.
normalized_question_text: A sound field is created by an arrangement of identical line sources grouped into two identical arrays of $N$ sources each as shown (Fig. 2.62). All of the radiators lie in a plane perpendicular to the page, and produce waves of wavelength $\theta, N, c$ and $d$, the distance between the centers of the arrays.
reasoning_chain:
The intensity distribution produced by each array is represented by
$$
I \sim \left(\frac{\sin \frac{N \delta}{2}}{\sin \frac{\delta}{2}}\right)^{2},
$$
where $\delta = 2\pi c \sin \theta / \lambda$. The expression
$$
I = I_{1} + I_{2} + 2\sqrt{I_{1} I_{2}} \cos \varphi
$$
for the resultant intensity of two coherent sources of phase difference $\varphi$ gives
$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= 2 I_{1}(1 + \cos \varphi) \\
&= 4 I_{1} \cos^{2}(\varphi / 2) \\
&\sim 4\left(\frac{\sin \frac{N \delta}{2}}{\sin \frac{\delta}{2}}\right)^{2} \cos^{2}\left(\frac{\pi d \sin \theta}{\lambda}\right),
\end{aligned}
$$
as $\varphi = \frac{3\pi d \sin \theta}{\lambda}$. The maximum intensity is given by $\frac{\delta}{2} = 0$, so that $I_{m} \sim 4 N^{2}$. Hence
$$
I = \frac{I_{m}}{N^{2}}\left(\frac{\sin \frac{N \delta}{2}}{\sin \frac{\delta}{2}}\right)^{2} \cos^{2}\left(\frac{\pi d \sin \theta}{\lambda}\right).
$$

raw_question_text: Three bodies of equal mass $m$ and indicated by $i=1,2,3$ are constrained to perform small oscillations along different coplanar axes forming $120^{\circ}$ angles at their common intersection, as shown in Fig. 1.94. Identical coupling springs hold these bodies near equilibrium positions which are at a distance $l$ from the intersection on each axis, that is, the equilibrium length of each spring is $\sqrt{3} l$. Show that the remaining normal modes are degenerate and determine their frequency.
normalized_question_text: Three bodies of equal mass $m$ and indicated by $i=1,2,3$ are constrained to perform small oscillations along different coplanar axes forming $120^{\circ}$ angles at their common intersection, as shown in Fig. 1.94. Identical coupling springs hold these bodies near equilibrium positions which are at a distance $l$ from the intersection on each axis, that is, the equilibrium length of each spring is $\sqrt{3} l$. Show that the remaining normal modes are degenerate and determine their frequency.
reasoning_chain: The remaining two normal modes are orthogonal to the above symmetric mode. They satisfy the condition $x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}=0$, for which the equations of motion reduce to $m\ddot{x}_{i}=-K x_{i}$. Setting $x_{i}=b_{i}\cos(\omega' t+\varphi')$, we have $\sum_{i}b_{i}=0$ and $\omega'^{2}=\frac{K}{m}$. The frequency is the same for both modes, hence they are degenerate.

raw_question_text: Figure 2.67 shows a simplified electron lens consisting of a circular loop (of radius $a$) of wire carrying current $I$. For $\rho \ll a$ the vector potential is approximately given by $A_{\theta}=rac{\pi I a^{2} \rho}{\left(a^{2}+z^{2}\right)^{3/2}}$. normalized_question_text: Figure 2.67 shows a simplified electron lens consisting of a circular loop (of radius $a$) of wire carrying current $I$. For $\rho \ll a$ the vector potential is approximately given by $A_{\theta}=rac{\pi I a^{2} \rho}{\left(a^{2}+z^{2}\right)^{3/2}}$. Show that the canonical momentum $p_{\theta}$ vanishes for the orbit shown and find an expression for $\dot{\theta}$.
reasoning_chain: Using Hamilton's canonical equation $\dot{P}_{\theta}=-\frac{\partial H}{\partial \theta}$, we obtain $\dot{P}_{\theta}=0$, i.e., $P_{\theta}=m\rho^{2}\dot{\theta}+\frac{I\pi a^{2}q\rho^{2}}{c(a^{2}+z^{2})^{3/2}}=\text{const}$. Initially, when the particle was far away from the lens ($\rho=0$) with $v_{\theta}=0$, we have $P_{\theta}=0$, yielding $\dot{\theta}=-\frac{I\pi a^{2}q}{mc(a^{2}+z^{2})^{3/2}}$.

raw_question_text: Refer to Fig. 3.25. The switch is suddenly moved to position $B$. Just after the switching, what are currents in $\varepsilon_{2}$, $R_{1}$, $R_{2}$ and $L$ ?

raw_question_text: Suppose the radiation is observed far from the charges at an angle $\theta$ relative to the axis of rotation. What is the polarization for $\theta=0^{\circ}$, $90^{\circ}$, and $0<\theta<90^{\circ}$?
normalized_question_text: Suppose the radiation is observed far from the charges at an angle $\theta$ relative to the axis of rotation. What is the polarization for $\theta=0^{\circ}$, $90^{\circ}$, and $0<\theta<90^{\circ}$?
raw_answer_text: For $\theta=0^{\circ}$, no radiation is emitted; for $\theta=90^{\circ}$, the radiation is circularly polarized; for $0^{\circ}<\theta<90^{\circ}$, the radiation is not polarized.
normalized_answer_text: For $\theta=0^{\circ}$, no radiation is emitted; for $\theta=90^{\circ}$, the radiation is circularly polarized; for $0^{\circ}<\theta<90^{\circ}$, the radiation is not polarized.
reasoning_chain: At a far point $\mathbf{r}(r,\theta,\varphi)$ the magnetic induction of the radiation field is given by $\mathbf{B}=-i\frac{\mu_{0}}{4\pi}\frac{\omega k}{6r}\mathbf{k}\times\mathbf{Q}$ with $k=\frac{\omega}{c}\mathbf{e}_{r}$ and $\mathbf{Q}$ having components $Q_{i}=\frac{1}{r}\sum_{j}Q_{ij}x_{j}$. For $\theta=0^{\circ}$, one finds $Q_{1}=Q_{2}=0$, so that $\mathbf{B}=0$ (no radiation). For $\theta=90^{\circ}$, with $Q_{1}\sim 3el^{2}e^{-i(\omega t'-\varphi)}$, $Q_{2}\sim 3el^{2}ie^{-i(\omega t'-\varphi)}$, and $\mathbf{e}_{r}=(\cos\varphi,\sin\varphi,0)$, the resulting field yields circular polarization. For $0^{\circ}<\theta<90^{\circ}$, analysis of the components $B_{x}$, $B_{y}$, and $B_{z}$ shows that all are time-dependent, so the radiation is not polarized.

raw_question_text: A marble bounces down stairs in a regular manner, hitting each step at the same place and bouncing the same height above each step (Fig. 1.5). The stair height equals its depth (tread=rise) and the coefficient of restitution $e$ is given. Find the necessary horizontal velocity and bounce height (the coefficient of restitution is defined as $e=-v_{f} / v_{i}$, where $v_{f}$ and $v_{i}$ are the vertical velocities just after and before the bounce respectively).
normalized_question_text: A marble bounces down stairs in a regular manner, hitting each step at the same place and bouncing the same height above each step (Fig. 1.5). The stair height equals its depth (tread = rise) and the coefficient of restitution $e$ is given. Find the necessary horizontal velocity and bounce height. The coefficient of restitution is defined as $e = -v_f / v_i$, where $v_f$ and $v_i$ are the vertical velocities just after and before the bounce, respectively.
reasoning_chain: Use unit vectors $\mathbf{i}, \mathbf{j}$ as shown in Fig. 1.5 and let the horizontal velocity of the marble be $v_{h}$. The velocities just before and after a bounce are respectively $\mathbf{v}_{1}=v_{h}\mathbf{i}+v_{i}\mathbf{j}$ and $\mathbf{v}_{2}=v_{h}\mathbf{i}+v_{f}\mathbf{j}$. As the conditions at each step remain exactly the same, $v_{i}$, $v_{f}$ and $v_{h}$ are all constant. The conservation of mechanical energy $\frac{1}{2}mv_{1}^{2}=\frac{1}{2}mv_{2}^{2}+mgl$ gives $v_{i}^{2}=v_{f}^{2}+2gl$. As by definition $v_{f}=-ev_{i}$, the above gives $v_{i}^{2}=\frac{2gl}{1-e^{2}}$. The time required for each bounce is $t=\frac{v_{i}-v_{f}}{g}=\frac{l}{v_{h}}$, giving $v_{h}=\frac{gl}{v_{i}-v_{f}}=\frac{gl}{(1+e)v_{i}}=\sqrt{\frac{gl}{2}\frac{1-e}{1+e}}$.

raw_question_text: A coin spinning about its axis of symmetry with angular frequency $\omega$ is set down on a horizontal surface (Fig. 1.148). After it stops slipping, with what velocity does it roll away?
normalized_question_text: A coin spinning about its axis of symmetry with angular frequency $\omega$ is set down on a horizontal surface. After it stops slipping, with what velocity does it roll away?
reasoning_chain: Take coordinates as shown in Fig. 1.149. Before the coin stops slipping, the frictional force is $f=\mu mg$, where $\mu$ is the coefficient of sliding friction. Let $x_{c}$ be the $x$ coordinate of the center of mass of the coin. The equations of motion of the coin before it stops slipping are $$m\ddot{x}_{c}=-\mu mg$$ and $$I\ddot{\theta}=-\mu mgR,$$ where $m$ and $R$ are respectively the mass and radius of the coin, and $I=\frac{1}{2}mR^{2}$. Integrating and using initial conditions $\dot{x}_{c}=0$, $\dot{\theta}=\omega$ at $t=0$, we have $$\dot{x}_{c}=-\mu gt$$ and $$\dot{\theta}=\omega-\frac{2\mu gt}{R}.$$ When the coin rolls without slipping, we have $$\dot{x}_{c}=-\dot{\theta}R.$$ Suppose this happens at time $t$, then the above give $$-\mu gt=-\omega R+2\mu gt$$ or $$t=\frac{\omega R}{3\mu g}.$$ At this time, the velocity of the center of mass of the coin is $$\dot{x}_{c}=-\mu gt=-\frac{1}{3}\omega R,$$ which is the velocity with which the coin rolls away without slipping.

raw_question_text: A plane wave of light from a laser has a wavelength of $6000 \AA$. The light is incident on a double slit. After passing through the double slit the light falls on a screen 100 cm beyond the double slit. The intensity distribution of the interference pattern on the screen is shown (Fig. 2.48). What is the width of each slit and what is the separation of the two slits?
normalized_question_text: A plane wave of light from a laser has a wavelength of $6000 \AA$. The light is incident on a double slit. After passing through the double slit, the light falls on a screen 100 cm beyond the double slit. The intensity distribution of the interference pattern on the screen is shown in Fig. 2.48. What is the width of each slit and what is the separation of the two slits?
reasoning_chain:
$$
\text{Solution:}
$$

For double-slit interference
$$
I \sim \left(\frac{\sin \beta}{\beta}\right)^2 \cos^2 \gamma
$$
where
$$
\beta = \frac{\pi b \sin \theta}{\lambda}, \quad \gamma = \frac{\pi d \sin \theta}{\lambda}, \quad \text{with} \quad \sin \theta \approx \frac{y}{D}
$$
$b$ being the width of each slit, $d$ the separation of their centers, $D$ the distance between the screen and the double slit, and $\lambda$ the wavelength of the incident light.

The minima are given by $\gamma = \left(n + \frac{1}{2}\right)\pi$, $n = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$ Thus the fringe spacing $\Delta y$ is given by
$$
\Delta y = \frac{\Delta \gamma}{\pi} \frac{\lambda D}{d} = \frac{\lambda D}{d}.
$$

Hence, for $\Delta y = 1\,\text{cm}$ as from Fig.\,2.48,
$$
d = \frac{\lambda D}{\Delta y} = 6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{cm}.
$$

The pattern shows the missing order $d/b = 4$, from which we get the width of each slit,
$$
b = \frac{d}{4} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{cm}.
$$

raw_question_text: The figure 2.21 shows the cross section of an infinitely long circular cylinder of radius $3a$ with an infinitely long cylindrical hole of radius $a$ displaced so that its center is at a distance $a$ from the center of the big cylinder. The solid part of the cylinder carries a current $I$, distributed uniformly over the cross section, and out from the plane of the paper. Find the magnetic field at all points on the plane $P$ containing the axes of the cylinders.
normalized_question_text: The figure 2.21 shows the cross section of an infinitely long circular cylinder of radius $3a$ with an infinitely long cylindrical hole of radius $a$ displaced so that its center is at a distance $a$ from the center of the big cylinder. The solid part of the cylinder carries a current $I$, distributed uniformly over the cross section, and out from the plane of the paper. Find the magnetic field at all points on the plane $P$ containing the axes of the cylinders.
raw_answer_text: Inside the hole ($0<x<2a$): $H_y=\frac{I}{16\pi a}$; Inside the solid part ($2a\le x\le3a$ or $-3a\le x\le0$): $H_y=\frac{I(x^2-ax-a^2)}{16\pi a^2(x-a)}$; Outside the cylinder ($|x|>3a$): $H_y=\frac{(8x-9a)I}{16\pi x(x-a)}$
normalized_answer_text: Inside the hole ($0<x<2a$): $H_y=\frac{I}{16\pi a}$; Inside the solid part ($2a\le x\le3a$ or $-3a\le x\le0$): $H_y=\frac{I(x^2-ax-a^2)}{16\pi a^2(x-a)}$; Outside the cylinder ($|x|>3a$): $H_y=\frac{(8x-9a)I}{16\pi x(x-a)}$
reasoning_chain: According to the principle of superposition the field can be regarded as the difference of two fields $\mathbf{H}_{2}$ and $\mathbf{H}_{1}$, where $\mathbf{H}_{2}$ is the field produced by a solid (without the hole) cylinder of radius $3a$ and $\mathbf{H}_{1}$ is that produced by a cylinder of radius $a$ at the position of the hole. The current in each of these two cylinders is uniformly distributed over the cross section. The currents $I_{1}$ and $I_{2}$ in the small and large cylinders have current densities $-j$ and $+j$ respectively. Then as $I=I_{2}-I_{1}=9\pi a^{2}j-\pi a^{2}j=8\pi a^{2}j$, we have $j=\frac{I}{8\pi a^{2}}$ and $I_{1}=\pi a^{2}j=\frac{I}{8}$, $I_{2}=9\pi a^{2}j=\frac{9}{8}I$. Take the $z$-axis along the axis of the large cylinder with its positive direction in the direction of $I_{2}$, which we assume to be upwards from the plane of the paper. Take the $x$-axis crossing the axis of the small cylinder as shown in Fig. 2.21. Then the plane $P$ is the $xz$ plane, i.e., the plane $y=0$. Ampere's law gives $H_{1}$ and $H_{2}$ as follows (noting $r=\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}$, $r_{1}=\sqrt{(x-a)^{2}+y^{2}}$): for $r\le3a$, $H_{2x}=-\frac{Iy}{16\pi a^{2}}$, $H_{2y}=\frac{Ix}{16\pi a^{2}}$, and for $r>3a$, $H_{2x}=-\frac{9Iy}{16\pi(x^{2}+y^{2})}$, $H_{2y}=\frac{9Ix}{16\pi(x^{2}+y^{2})}$. Similarly, for $r_{1}\le a$, $H_{1x}=-\frac{Iy}{16\pi a^{2}}$, $H_{1y}=\frac{I(x-a)}{16\pi a^{2}}$, and for $r_{1}>a$, $H_{1x}=-\frac{Iy}{16\pi[(x-a)^{2}+y^{2}]}$, $H_{1y}=\frac{I(x-a)}{16\pi[(x-a)^{2}+y^{2}]}$. On the plane $P$, $H_{2x}=H_{1x}=0$, hence $H_{x}=0$, $H_{y}=H_{2y}-H_{1y}$. We therefore have: (1) Inside the hole ($0<x<2a$), $H_{y}=\frac{Ix}{16\pi a^{2}}-\frac{I(x-a)}{16\pi a^{2}}=\frac{I}{16\pi a}$. (2) Inside the solid part ($2a\le x\le3a$ or $-3a\le x\le0$), $H_{y}=\frac{Ix}{16\pi a^{2}}-\frac{I(x-a)}{16\pi[(x-a)^{2}+y^{2}]}=\frac{I(x^{2}-ax-a^{2})}{16\pi a^{2}(x-a)}$. (3) Outside the cylinder ($|x|>3a$), $H_{y}=\frac{9Ix}{16\pi(x^{2}+y^{2})}-\frac{I(x-a)}{16\pi[(x-a)^{2}+y^{2}]}=\frac{(8x-9a)I}{16\pi x(x-a)}$.

raw_question_text: Flat metallic plates $P, P^{\prime}$, and $P^{\prime \prime}$ (see Fig. 1.53) are vertical and the plate $P$, of mass $M$, is free to move vertically between $P^{\prime}$ and $P^{\prime \prime}$. raw_question_text: As in Fig. 4.14 an electromagnetic wave is propagating in the TE mode in the rectangular waveguide. The walls of the waveguide are conducting and the inside is vacuum. What is the cutoff frequency in this mode?
normalized_question_text: As in Fig. 4.14, an electromagnetic wave is propagating in the TE mode in the rectangular waveguide. The walls of the waveguide are conducting and the inside is vacuum. What is the cutoff frequency in this mode?
reasoning_chain: In TE modes $E_{z}=0$, $H_{z}\neq 0$, using the coordinate system shown in Fig. 4.14. The transverse, i.e. $x$ and $y$, component waves in the waveguide are standing waves, while the $z$ component is a traveling wave. Let $m$ and $n$ denote the numbers of half-waves in the $x$ and $y$ directions respectively. The wave numbers of the standing waves are then $k_{x}=\frac{m \pi}{b}, \quad k_{y}=\frac{n \pi}{a}$ while the wave number of the traveling wave is $k_{z}^{2}=k^{2}-(k_{x}^{2}+k_{y}^{2})$, where $k^{2}=\mu\varepsilon\omega^{2}$. (a) If the inside of the waveguide is vacuum, we have $k^{2}=\mu_{0}\varepsilon_{0}\omega^{2}$ or $k_{x}^{2}=\mu_{0}\varepsilon_{0}\omega^{2}-\left[\left(\frac{m \pi}{b}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{n \pi}{a}\right)^{2}\right]$. If $k_{z}^{2}<0$, $k_{z}$ is purely imaginary and the traveling wave becomes exponentially attenuating, i.e., no propagation. Hence the cutoff frequency is given by $\omega_{mn}=\frac{\pi}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{0}\mu}}\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{b}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{n}{a}\right)^{2}}$.

raw_question_text: Switch S is thrown to position A as shown in Fig. 3.30. Find the magnitude and direction ("up" or "down" along page) of the currents in $R_{1}, R_{2}$, and $R_{3}$, after the switch has been in position $A$ for several seconds.
normalized_question_text: Switch S is thrown to position A as shown in Fig. 3.30. Find the magnitude and direction ("up" or "down" along the page) of the currents in $R_1$, $R_2$, and $R_3$ after the switch has been in position A for several seconds.
reasoning_chain: $i_{1}(0)=i_{2}(0)=\frac{2}{R_{1}+R_{2}}=\frac{2}{3+2}=0.4\,\mathrm{A}$, $i_{3}(0)=0$, $i_{1}(\infty)=\frac{2}{R_{1}+R_{2}\| R_{3}}=0.59\,\mathrm{A}$. Then, after a long time, $i_{2}(\infty)=\frac{R_{3}}{R_{2}+R_{3}}\,i_{1}(\infty)=0.12\,\mathrm{A}$ and $i_{3}(\infty)=\frac{R_{2}}{R_{2}+R_{3}}\,i_{1}(\infty)=0.47\,\mathrm{A}$. The resistance seen is $R=R_{3}+R_{1}\| R_{2}=1.7\,\Omega$ and the time constant is $\tau=\frac{L_{1}}{R}=\frac{5}{1.7}=\frac{1}{0.34}\,\mathrm{s}$. Using $i(t)=i(\infty)+[i(0)-i(\infty)]e^{-t/\tau}$, we get $i_{1}(t)=0.59-0.19e^{-0.34t}\,\mathrm{A}$ (upward), $i_{2}(t)=0.12+0.28e^{-0.34t}\,\mathrm{A}$ (downward), and $i_{3}(t)=0.47(1-e^{-0.34t})\,\mathrm{A}$ (downward).

raw_question_text: Three bodies of equal mass $m$ and indicated by $i=1,2,3$ are constrained to perform small oscillations along different coplanar axes forming $120^{\circ}$ angles at their common intersection, as shown in Fig. 1.94. Identical coupling springs hold these bodies near equilibrium positions which are at a distance $l$ from the intersection on each axis, that is, the equilibrium length of each spring is $\sqrt{3} l$. Show that the equations of motion of the three bodies are represented by the coupled system $\frac{m d^{2} x_{i}}{d t^{2}}=-K x_{i}-k\left(x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}\right)$, where $x_{i}(t)+l$ indicates their respective distances from the intersection. (Specifically, both $K$ and $k$ equal $3 / 4$ of each spring constant.)
normalized_question_text: Three bodies of equal mass $m$, labeled $i=1,2,3$, are constrained to perform small oscillations along different coplanar axes forming $120^{\circ}$ angles at their common intersection, as shown in Fig. 1.94. Identical coupling springs hold these bodies near equilibrium positions that are at a distance $l$ from the intersection on each axis, so the equilibrium length of each spring is $\sqrt{3}l$. Show that the equations of motion of the three bodies are represented by the coupled system $\frac{m d^{2} x_{i}}{d t^{2}}=-K x_{i}-k\left(x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}\right)$, where $x_{i}(t)+l$ indicates the respective distance from the intersection. Specifically, both $K$ and $k$ equal $3/4$ of each spring constant.
reasoning_chain: Let the constant of each spring be $\eta$ and consider particles $i$ and $j$ which are located at $x_{i}$ and $x_{j}$ from their respective equilibrium positions. The stretch of the spring between the two particles is $(x_{i}+x_{j})\cos30^{\circ}$, so the potential energy of the system is $U=\frac{3\eta}{8}\left[(x_{1}+x_{2})^{2}+(x_{2}+x_{3})^{2}+(x_{3}+x_{1})^{2}\right]$. The force acting on the $i$th particle is then $-\frac{\partial U}{\partial x_{i}}=-\frac{3\eta}{4}\left(x_{i}+\sum_{j}x_{j}\right)$, giving its equation of motion as $m\ddot{x}_{i}=-K x_{i}-k(x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3})$ with $K=k=\frac{3\eta}{4}$.

(Hint: The lowest mode has the electric field in the $z$-direction only.)

The walls are perfect conductors. Write the wave equation that describes the $E$ and $B$ fields of the lowest mode. (Hint: The lowest mode has the electric field in the $z$-direction only.)
raw_answer_text: The electric field satisfies $\nabla^{2}E_{z}+k^{2}E_{z}=0$, with $E_{x}=E_{y}=0$, and the magnetic field is given by $B_{x}=-\frac{i}{\omega}\frac{\partial E_{z}}{\partial y}$, $B_{y}=\frac{i}{\omega}\frac{\partial E_{z}}{\partial x}$, and $B_{z}=0$.
normalized_answer_text: The electric field satisfies $\nabla^{2}E_{z}+k^{2}E_{z}=0$, with $E_{x}=E_{y}=0$, and the magnetic field is given by $B_{x}=-\frac{i}{\omega}\frac{\partial E_{z}}{\partial y}$, $B_{y}=\frac{i}{\omega}\frac{\partial E_{z}}{\partial x}$, and $B_{z}=0$.
reasoning_chain:
For sinusoidal waves of angular frequency $\omega$, the wave equation reduces to Helmholtz's equation $$
abla^{2}E+k^{2}E=0,$$ with $$k^{2}= rac{\omega^{2}}{c^{2}},$$ and Maxwell's equation $$
abla	imes\mathbf{E}=- rac{\partial \mathbf{B}}{\partial t}$$ reduces to $$\mathbf{B}=- rac{i}{\omega}
abla	imes\mathbf{E}. $$ For the lowest mode, $E_{x}=E_{y}=0$ and $E=E_{z}$, so it is a TE wave given by $$
abla^{2}E_{z}+k^{2}E_{z}=0.$$ The magnetic field components then are $$B_{x}=- rac{i}{\omega} rac{\partial E_{z}}{\partial y},\quad B_{y}= rac{i}{\omega} rac{\partial E_{z}}{\partial x},\quad B_{z}=0.$$

raw_question_text: An ultra-high speed rotor consists of a homogeneous disc of mass $M$, radius $R$ and width $2l$. It is mounted on a shaft supported on bearings separated by a distance $2d$ as shown in Fig. 1.123. The two additional masses, of equal mass $m$, are arranged symmetrically so that the rotor remains in "static" balance. Find the time-varying force on the bearings if the rotor turns at angular velocity $\omega$. (Wisconsin)
normalized_question_text: An ultra-high speed rotor consists of a homogeneous disc of mass $M$, radius $R$ and width $2l$. It is mounted on a shaft supported on bearings separated by a distance $2d$ as shown in Fig. 1.123. The two additional masses, of equal mass $m$, are arranged symmetrically so that the rotor remains in "static" balance. Find the time-varying force on the bearings if the rotor turns at angular velocity $\omega$.
reasoning_chain: In the rotating frame attached to the disk, the additional masses each suffers a centrifugal force $mR\omega^{2}$, resulting in a torque $T=2mR\omega^{2}l$. This torque is balanced by a torque of the same magnitude but opposite in direction, supplied by the bearings which are separated by a distance $2d$. Hence the bearings each suffers a force $\frac{T}{2d}=\frac{mR\omega^{2}l}{d}$ in the same direction as that of the centrifugal force on the nearer mass. In the fixed frame these rotate with angular velocity $\omega$.

raw_question_text: The circuit shown in Fig. 3.61 is a relaxation oscillator built from an ideal (infinite open‐loop gain, infinite input impedance) differential amplifier. The amplifier saturates at an output of $\pm 10\,V$. Calculate the frequency of oscillation for the component values given.
normalized_question_text: The circuit shown in Fig. 3.61 is a relaxation oscillator built from an ideal differential amplifier (infinite open-loop gain, infinite input impedance). The amplifier saturates at an output of $\pm 10\,V$. Calculate the frequency of oscillation for the component values given.
reasoning_chain: Solution: (a) This is a relaxation oscillator having positive feedback shunted by $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$ and discharged through an $RC$ circuit. When stabiliity is reached, the output is a rectangular wave with amplitude equal to the saturated voltage. Let $V_{C}=+10\,V$. The potential at point B is $V_{B}=\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1}+R_{2}}\times V_{C}=2\,V$. The capacitor C is charged through $R$, and $V_{A}$ will increase from -2 V to +2 V. When $V_{A}$ is higher than $V_{B}$, $V_{C}$ will decrease to -10 V and C will discharge through $R$. When $V_{A}$ is lower than the potential at B, which is now $\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1}+R_{2}}\times (-10)=-2\,V$, $V_{C}$ will again increase to +10 V. So following each charging the circuit relaxes back to the starting point, i.e., relaxation oscillation occurs. The charging of the capacitor follows $V=V_{0}[1-\exp(-t/RC)]$, or $t=RC\ln\left(\frac{V_{0}}{V_{0}-V}\right)$. The charging time $T$ from $V_{1}$ to $V_{2}$ is $T=RC\ln\frac{V_{0}-V_{1}}{V_{0}-V_{2}}$. The charging time is given by $V_{0}=10\,V$, $V_{1}=-2\,V$, $V_{2}=2\,V$, i.e., $T_{1}=RC\ln\frac{10+2}{10-2}=8.1\,ms$; the discharging time is given by $V_{0}=-10\,V$, $V_{1}=2\,V$, $V_{2}=-2\,V$, i.e., $T_{2}=RC\ln\frac{-10-2}{-10+2}=8.1\,ms$. Finally, the frequency of oscillation is calculated as $f=1/(T_{1}+T_{2})=61.7\,Hz$.

raw_question_text: As shown in Fig. 3.26, the switch has been in position A for a long time. At $t=0$ it is suddenly moved to position B. Immediately after contact with B: Between $t=0$ and $t=0.1 \mathrm{~s}$, what total energy is dissipated in $R$ ?

raw_question_text: A cord passing over a frictionless pulley has a 9 kg mass tied on one end and a 7 kg mass on the other end (Fig. 1.2). Determine the acceleration of the cord.

raw_question_text: A toroid having an iron core of square cross section (Fig. 2.17) and permeability $\mu$ is wound with $N$ closely spaced turns of wire carrying a current $I$. Find the magnitude of the magnetization $M$ everywhere inside the iron.
normalized_question_text: A toroid having an iron core of square cross section and permeability $\mu$ is wound with $N$ closely spaced turns of wire carrying a current $I$. Find the magnitude of the magnetization $M$ everywhere inside the iron.
reasoning_chain: According to Ampère's circuital law $\oint \mathbf{H}\cdot d\mathbf{l}=NI$, we have $H=\frac{NI}{2\pi r}$ where $r$ is the distance from the axis of the toroid. The magnetization $M$ inside the iron is then obtained as $M=\frac{B}{\mu_{0}}-H=\frac{\mu H}{\mu_{0}}-H=\frac{\mu-1}{\mu_{0}}\cdot\frac{NI}{2\pi r}$.

raw_question_text: Consider the interference pattern from the three slits illustrated (Fig. 2.3). Assume the openings of the individual slits are the same ($\leqslant \frac{\lambda}{2}$). At what value of $\theta$ is the first principal maximum? (i.e., the wavelets from all three slits are in phase).
normalized_question_text: Consider the interference pattern from the three slits illustrated in Fig. 2.3. Assume the openings of the individual slits are the same ($\leqslant \frac{\lambda}{2}$). At what value of $\theta$ is the first principal maximum (i.e., the wavelets from all three slits are in phase)?
reasoning_chain:
The electric field $E(\theta)$ on the screen is the sum of the fields produced by the slits individually:

$$
E(\theta) = E_{1}' + E_{2} + E_{3} = A + A e^{i\delta} + A e^{i\frac{3}{2}\delta}
$$

where
$$
\delta = \frac{2\pi d}{\lambda} \sin\theta
$$

Then the total intensity at $\theta$ is
$$
I(\theta) \sim E(\theta) \cdot E^{*}(\theta) = A^{2} \left\{3 + 2\left[\cos\delta + \cos\left(\frac{3\delta}{2}\right) + \cos\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right)\right]\right\}
$$

For $\theta = 0$, we have
$$
I(0) \sim 9 A^{2}
$$

The expression for $I(\theta)$ shows that the first principal maximum occurs at $\delta = 4\pi$, i.e.,
$$
\left(\frac{2\pi d}{\lambda}\right) \sin\theta_{1} = 4\pi
$$

or
$$
\theta_{1} \approx \sin\theta_{1} \sim \frac{2\lambda}{d}

raw_question_text: A conducting loop of area $A$ and total resistance $R$ is suspended by a torsion spring of constant $k$ in a uniform magnetic field $\mathbf{B}=B\,\mathbf{e}_{\mathbf{y}}$. The loop is in the $yz$ plane at equilibrium and can rotate about the $z$-axis with moment of inertia $I$ as shown in Fig. 2.74(a). The loop is displaced by small angle $\theta$ from equilibrium and released. Assume the torsion spring is non-conducting and neglect self-inductance of the loop. What is the equation of motion for the loop in terms of the given parameters?
normalized_question_text: A conducting loop of area $A$ and total resistance $R$ is suspended by a torsion spring of constant $k$ in a uniform magnetic field $\mathbf{B}=B\,\mathbf{e}_{\mathbf{y}}$. The loop is in the $yz$ plane at equilibrium and can rotate about the $z$-axis with moment of inertia $I$ as shown in Fig. 2.74(a). The loop is displaced by small angle $\theta$ from equilibrium and released. Assume the torsion spring is non-conducting and neglect self-inductance of the loop. What is the equation of motion for the loop in terms of the given parameters?
reasoning_chain: when the angle between the plane of the loop and the magnetic field is $\alpha$, the magnetic flux passing through the loop is $\phi=B A \sin \alpha$. The induced emf and current are given by $\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=-B A \cos \alpha \dot{\alpha}$, $i=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=-\frac{B A \dot{\alpha} \cos \alpha}{R}$. The magnetic moment of the loop is $m=i A=-\frac{B A^{2} \cos \alpha}{R} \dot{\alpha}$. Thus the magnetic torque on the loop is $\tau_{m}=|m \times B|=-\frac{B^{2} A^{2} \cos ^{2}\alpha}{R} \dot{\alpha}$. Besides, the torsion spring also provides a twisting torque $k\alpha$. Both torques will resist the rotation of the loop. Thus one has $I\ddot{\alpha}+\frac{B^{2} A^{2}\cos ^{2} \alpha}{R}\dot{\alpha}+k\alpha=0$. As $\alpha\ll \theta$ and $\theta$ is itself small, we have $\cos ^{2}\alpha\approx 1$ and $I\ddot{\alpha}+\frac{B^{2} A^{2}}{R}\dot{\alpha}+k\alpha=0$. Let $\alpha=e^{Ct}$ and obtain the characteristic equation $IC^{2}+\frac{B^{2} A^{2}}{R}C+k=0$. The solution is $C=\frac{-\frac{B^{2} A^{2}}{R}\pm\sqrt{\left(\frac{B^{2} A^{2}}{R}\right)^{2}-4Ik}}{2I}=-\frac{B^{2} A^{2}}{2IR}\pm j\sqrt{\frac{k}{I}-\left(\frac{B^{2} A^{2}}{2IR}\right)^{2}}$. Defining $\beta=\frac{B^{2} A^{2}}{2IR}$ and $\gamma=\sqrt{-\left(\frac{B^{2} A^{2}}{2IR}\right)^{2}+\frac{k}{I}}$, we have two solutions $C_{1}=-\beta+j\gamma$ and $C_{2}=-\beta-j\gamma$. The general solution of the equation of motion is therefore $\alpha=e^{-\beta t}\left[A_{1}\cos\gamma t+A_{2}\sin\gamma t\right]$. Since $\alpha|_{t=0}=\theta$ and $\dot{\alpha}|_{t=0}=0$, we find $A_{1}=\theta$ and $A_{2}=\frac{\beta}{\gamma}\theta$. Hence the rotational oscillation of the loop is described by $\alpha(t)=\theta e^{-\beta t}\left[\cos\gamma t+\frac{\beta}{\gamma}\sin\gamma t\right]$.

raw_question_text: The elastic properties of a solid support elastic waves. Assume a transverse plane wave which proceeds in the $x$-direction and whose oscillations are in the $y$-direction. Derive the equations of motion for the displacement.
normalized_question_text: The elastic properties of a solid support elastic waves. Assume a transverse plane wave which proceeds in the x-direction and whose oscillations are in the y-direction. Derive the equation of motion for the displacement.
reasoning_chain: The potential energy of a unit volume of the block due to shear strain is $\frac{1}{lA}\int_{0}^{u}Fdu' = \frac{1}{lA}\int_{0}^{\varphi}An\varphi'l\,d\varphi' = \frac{1}{2}n\varphi^{2} = \frac{1}{2}n\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right)^{2}$. The kinetic energy of the block during shearing is $\int_{0}^{l}\frac{1}{2}\rho A\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}\right)^{2}\,dx$, $\rho$ being the density of the block. Within the elastic limits, energy is considered and Hamilton's principle $\delta\int_{t_{1}}^{t_{2}}L\,dt=0$ applies. Thus $\delta\int_{t_{1}}^{t_{2}}(T-V)\,dt = \delta\int_{t_{1}}^{t_{2}}\int_{0}^{l}\left[\frac{n}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right)^{2} - \frac{\rho}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}\right)^{2}\right]A\,dx\,dt = 0$. Integrating by parts, $\int_{0}^{l}\delta\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right)^{2}dx = 2\int_{0}^{l}\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right)\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(\delta u)dx = \left.2\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right)\delta u\right|_{0}^{l}-2\int_{0}^{l}\frac{\partial^{2}u}{\partial x^{2}}\delta u\,dx$, and $\int_{0}^{l}\delta\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}\right)^{2}dt = \left.2\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}\right)\delta u\right|_{t_{1}}^{t_{2}}-2\int_{0}^{l}\left(\frac{\partial^{2}u}{\partial t^{2}}\right)\delta u\,dt$. With $\delta u=0$ at $x=0,l$ and $t=t_{1},t_{2}$, this leads to $-\int_{t_{1}}^{t_{2}}\int_{0}^{l}\left(n\frac{\partial^{2}u}{\partial x^{2}}-\rho\frac{\partial^{2}u}{\partial t^{2}}\right)\delta u\,A\,dx\,dt=0$, giving $\frac{\partial^{2}u}{\partial x^{2}}-\frac{\rho}{n}\frac{\partial^{2}u}{\partial t^{2}}=0$.

raw_question_text: A bucket of water is rotated at a constant angular velocity $\omega$ about its symmetry axis. Determine the shape of the surface of the water after everything has settled down. (MIT)
normalized_question_text: A bucket of water is rotated at a constant angular velocity $\omega$ about its symmetry axis. Determine the shape of the surface of the water after everything has settled down. (MIT)
reasoning_chain: Consider a particle of water of mass $m$ at the surface. Two forces act on it: a force $\mathbf{F}$ normal to the surface due to neighboring water particles, and gravity $mg$, as shown in Fig. 1.239. As it moves in a circular orbit with constant angular velocity $\omega$, in a cylindrical coordinate system with origin at the lowest point of the surface we have $$ \begin{array}{l} F \cos \theta=mg \\ F \sin \theta=m\omega^{2}r \end{array} $$ where $\theta$ is the angle formed by the normal to the water surface and the $z$-axis. Hence $$ \tan \theta=\frac{\omega^{2} r}{g} $$ As $\tan \theta$ is the slope of the curve representing the shape of the surface, $$ \frac{dz}{dr}=\frac{\omega^{2}r}{g} $$ giving $$ z=\frac{\omega^{2}r^{2}}{2g} $$ as $z=0$ for $r=0$. Hence the surface is a paraboloid generated by rotating the above parabola about the $z$-axis.

raw_question_text: A disk of mass $M$ and radius $R$ slides without friction on a horizontal surface. Another disk of mass $m$ and radius $r$ is pinned through its center to a point off the center of the first disk by a distance $b$, so that it can rotate without friction on the first disk as shown in Fig. 2.2. Describe the motion and identify its constants.
normalized_question_text: A disk of mass $M$ and radius $R$ slides without friction on a horizontal surface. Another disk of mass $m$ and radius $r$ is pinned through its center to a point off the center of the first disk by a distance $b$, so that it can rotate without friction on the first disk as shown in Fig. 2.2. Describe the motion and identify its constants.
raw_answer_text: $(M+m)\dot{x}-m b \dot{\theta}\sin\theta=\text{ constant},\quad (M+m)\dot{y}+m b \dot{\theta}\cos\theta=\text{ constant},\quad \dot{\varphi}=\text{ constant},\quad T=\text{ constant},\quad \dot{\theta}=\text{ constant}$
normalized_answer_text: $(M+m)\dot{x}-m b \dot{\theta}\sin\theta=\text{ constant},\quad (M+m)\dot{y}+m b \dot{\theta}\cos\theta=\text{ constant},\quad \dot{\varphi}=\text{ constant},\quad T=\text{ constant},\quad \dot{\theta}=\text{ constant}$
reasoning_chain: Take generalized coordinates as follows: $x,y$, the coordinates of the center of mass of the larger disk, $\theta$, the angle of rotation of the larger disk and $\varphi$, the angle of rotation of the smaller disk as shown in Fig. 2.3. The center of mass of the smaller disk has coordinates $x+b\cos\theta$, $y+b\sin\theta$ and velocity components $\dot{x}-b\dot{\theta}\sin\theta$, $\dot{y}+b\dot{\theta}\cos\theta$. Hence the total kinetic energy of the system of the two disks is $T=\frac{1}{2}M(\dot{x}^{2}+\dot{y}^{2})+\frac{1}{4}MR^{2}\dot{\theta}^{2}+\frac{1}{2}m[(\dot{x}-b\dot{\theta}\sin\theta)^{2}+(\dot{y}+b\dot{\theta}\cos\theta)^{2}]+\frac{1}{4}mr^{2}\dot{\varphi}^{2}$ and the Lagrangian is $L=T=\frac{1}{2}M(\dot{x}^{2}+\dot{y}^{2})+\frac{1}{4}MR^{2}\dot{\theta}^{2}+\frac{1}{2}m[\dot{x}^{2}+\dot{y}^{2}+b^{2}\dot{\theta}^{2}-2b\dot{x}\dot{\theta}\sin\theta+2b\dot{y}\dot{\theta}\cos\theta]+\frac{1}{4}mr^{2}\dot{\varphi}^{2}$. Consider Lagrange's equations $\frac{d}{dt}(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_{i}})-\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_{i}}=0$. As $\frac{\partial L}{\partial x}=0$, we have $\frac{d}{dt}(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}})=0$, so $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}}=\text{ constant}$, or $(M+m)\dot{x}-mb\dot{\theta}\sin\theta=\text{ constant}$. Similarly, $\partial L/\partial y=0$ implies $(M+m)\dot{y}+mb\dot{\theta}\cos\theta=\text{ constant}$. Also, $\partial L/\partial \varphi=0$ leads to $\dot{\varphi}=\text{ constant}$. Finally, from $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta}=-mb\dot{x}\dot{\theta}\cos\theta-mb\dot{y}\dot{\theta}\sin\theta$ and $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}}=\frac{1}{2}MR^{2}\dot{\theta}+mb^{2}\dot{\theta}-mb\dot{x}\sin\theta+mb\dot{y}\cos\theta$, we obtain the equation of motion $\frac{1}{2}MR^{2}\ddot{\theta}+mb^{2}\ddot{\theta}-mb\ddot{x}\sin\theta+mb\ddot{y}\cos\theta=0$. Equations (1)-(4) describe the motion of the system. Since $V=0$ and $T+V=$ constant as there is no external force, the total kinetic energy of the system, $T$, is a conserved quantity. Conservation of the angular momentum about the center of mass of the system requires that, as $\dot{\varphi}=\text{ constant}$, $\dot{\theta}=\text{ constant}$.

raw_question_text: A sphere of mass $M$ and radius $R$ rolls without slipping down a triangular block of mass $m$ that is free to move on a frictionless horizontal surface, as shown in Fig. 2.18. Find the Lagrangian and state Lagrange's equations for this system subject to the force of gravity at the surface of the earth.
normalized_question_text: A sphere of mass $M$ and radius $R$ rolls without slipping down a triangular block of mass $m$ that is free to move on a frictionless horizontal surface, as shown in Fig. 2.18. Find the Lagrangian and state Lagrange's equations for this system subject to the force of gravity at the surface of the earth.
raw_answer_text: $L = \frac{1}{2} m \dot{x}^{2} + \frac{1}{2} M \left(\dot{x}^{2}+R^{2}\dot{\theta}^{2}+2R\dot{x}\dot{\theta}\cos \varphi\right) + \frac{1}{5} M R^{2}\dot{\theta}^{2} - M g (H-R\theta\sin \varphi)$, $(m+M)\ddot{x}+MR\ddot{\theta}\cos \varphi=0$, $\ddot{x}\cos \varphi+\frac{7}{5}R\ddot{\theta}-g\sin \varphi=0$
normalized_answer_text: $L = \frac{1}{2} m \dot{x}^{2} + \frac{1}{2} M \left(\dot{x}^{2}+R^{2}\dot{\theta}^{2}+2R\dot{x}\dot{\theta}\cos \varphi\right) + \frac{1}{5} M R^{2}\dot{\theta}^{2} - M g (H-R\theta\sin \varphi)$, $(m+M)\ddot{x}+MR\ddot{\theta}\cos \varphi=0$, $\ddot{x}\cos \varphi+\frac{7}{5}R\ddot{\theta}-g\sin \varphi=0$
reasoning_chain: Use a fixed coordinate frame as shown in Fig. 2.18 and let $\theta$ be the angle of rotation of the sphere. As the sphere rolls without slipping down the inclined plane, its center will have coordinates $\left(x+(\xi_{0}+R\theta)\cos \varphi, H-R\theta\sin \varphi\right)$ and velocity $(\dot{x}+R\dot{\theta}\cos \varphi, -R\dot{\theta}\sin \varphi)$. Note that at $t=0, x=0, \theta=0, \xi=\xi_{0}, y=H, \dot{x}=\dot{\theta}=0$. Then the Lagrangian is $L=T-V= \frac{1}{2} m \dot{x}^{2}+\frac{1}{2} M\left(\dot{x}^{2}+R^{2}\dot{\theta}^{2}+2R\dot{x}\dot{\theta}\cos \varphi\right)+\frac{1}{5} M R^{2}\dot{\theta}^{2}-M g(H-R\theta\sin \varphi)$. Lagrange's equations $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_{i}}\right)-\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_{i}}=0$ give $(m+M)\ddot{x}+M R\ddot{\theta}\cos \varphi=0$, $\ddot{x}\cos \varphi+\frac{7}{5} R\ddot{\theta}-g \sin \varphi=0$.

raw_question_text: Consider the Young's interference experiment shown in the diagram (Fig. 2.7). Assume that the wavelength of the light is $6000 \text{Å}$, the slit widths are all the same, $S_{0}=S_{1}=S_{2}=0.2 \text{mm}$, the slit separation $d=2.0 \text{mm}$, and $L_{1}=3.0 \text{m}$. About how long must $L_{0}$ be in order to produce a well-defined interference pattern on the screen?
normalized_question_text: Consider the Young's interference experiment shown in the diagram (Fig. 2.7). Assume that the wavelength of the light is $6000 \text{Å}$, the slit widths are all the same, $S_0=S_1=S_2=0.2\ \text{mm}$, the slit separation $d=2.0\ \text{mm}$, and $L_1=3.0\ \text{m}$. About how long must $L_0$ be in order to produce a well-defined interference pattern on the screen?
reasoning_chain:
According to the Van Cittert-Zernike theorem, the lateral coherence length of an extended slit source is given by
$$
l_{s} = \frac{\lambda}{\theta_{s}},
$$
where $\theta_{s}$ is the angle subtended by the slit at the point concerned in radians. 

In order to get a clear interference pattern, the distance between $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ should be less than $l_{s}$, i.e.,
$$
d < l_{s},
$$
or
$$
\theta_{s} < \frac{\lambda}{d}.
$$

As
$$
\theta_{s} = \frac{S_{0}}{L_{0}}
$$
we require that
$$
L_{0} > \frac{d}{\lambda} S_{0} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{6000 \times 10^{-10}} \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 0.67\,\text{m}.
$$

Using the equation $\mathbf{L}=\mathbf{r}\times\mathbf{p}$, calculate the angular momentum and show that it is equal to the answer for part (b).
reasoning_chain: The radius vectors of $M_1$ and $M_2$ from $O$ in the rotating frame are given by $$\mathbf{r}_1=A(\sin\theta,0,\cos\theta), \quad \mathbf{r}_2=A(-\sin\theta,0,-\cos\theta).$$ Using the transformation for the unit vectors, one obtains in the laboratory frame $$\mathbf{r}_1=A\left[\sin\theta\Big(\mathbf{e}_1'\cos\omega t+\mathbf{e}_2'\sin\omega t\Big)+\mathbf{e}_3'\cos\theta\right]=A(\sin\theta\cos\omega t, \sin\theta\sin\omega t, \cos\theta),$$ $$\mathbf{r}_2=A(-\sin\theta\cos\omega t,-\sin\theta\sin\omega t,-\cos\theta).$$ The angular momentum is then computed as $$\mathbf{L}=\sum\mathbf{r}\times\mathbf{p}=\sum M\mathbf{r}\times(\boldsymbol{\omega}\times\mathbf{r})=\sum M\left[r^2\boldsymbol{\omega}-(\mathbf{r}\cdot\boldsymbol{\omega})\mathbf{r}\right],$$ which leads to $$2MA^2\omega\mathbf{e}_3' - MA^2\omega\cos\theta\Big(\mathbf{e}_1'\sin\theta\cos\omega t+\mathbf{e}_2'\sin\theta\sin\omega t+\mathbf{e}_3'\cos\theta\Big)+MA^2\omega\cos\theta\Big(-\mathbf{e}_1'\sin\theta\cos\omega t-\mathbf{e}_2'\sin\theta\sin\omega t-\mathbf{e}_3'\cos\theta\Big]$$ and simplifies to $$\mathbf{L}=MA^2\omega\Big(-\mathbf{e}_1'\sin2\theta\cos\omega t-\mathbf{e}_2'\sin2\theta\sin\omega t+\mathbf{e}_3'2\sin^2\theta\Big),$$ which is the same as that obtained in part (b).

raw_question_text: Gas, in equilibrium at pressure $p_{0}$ and mass density $\rho_{0}$, is confined to a cylinder of length $L$ and cross sectional area $A$. The right hand end of the cylinder is closed and fixed. At the left hand end there is a frictionless and massless movable piston. In equilibrium the external force that must be exerted on the piston is of course $f_{0}=p_{0} A$. However, suppose a small additional force is supplied by an external agency: the harmonic force $f(t)=f_{0} \cos (\omega t)$. This produces small motions of the piston and thus small amplitude disturbances in the gas. Let $c$ be the speed of sound in the gas; neglect viscosity. Let $v(t)$ be the velocity of the piston. Compute $v(t)$.
normalized_question_text: Gas, in equilibrium at pressure $p_{0}$ and mass density $\rho_{0}$, is confined to a cylinder of length $L$ and cross sectional area $A$. The right hand end of the cylinder is closed and fixed. At the left hand end there is a frictionless and massless movable piston. In equilibrium the external force that must be exerted on the piston is of course $f_{0}=p_{0} A$. However, suppose a small additional force is supplied by an external agency: the harmonic force $f(t)=f_{0} \cos (\omega t)$. This produces small motions of the piston and thus small amplitude disturbances in the gas. Let $c$ be the speed of sound in the gas; neglect viscosity. Let $v(t)$ be the velocity of the piston. Compute $v(t)$.
reasoning_chain: Consider the gas as an ideal fluid. We choose a coordinate system whose origin is the equilibrium point (as shown in Fig. 1.46). Let the velocity of the macroscope motion of the gas be $v(x, t)$ and the pressure of the gas be $p(x, t)$. Because the displacement of the piston is very small, we can solve $v(x, t)$ and $p(x, t)$ approximately in the region $0 \leq x \leq L$ and consider $v(0, t)$. The boundary conditions are $p(0, t)=f(t) / A$ and $v(L, t)=0$. As $f(t)$ is a sinusoidal function of $t$ and the frequency is $\omega$, the resulting $v(x, t)$ and $p(x, t)$ must be waves of frequency $\omega$ and wave vector $k=\omega / c$. In fact, $v(x, t)$ and $p(x, t)$ both satisfy the wave equation with propagating velocity $c$. We can write $$\begin{array}{l} f(g)=\operatorname{Re} f_{0} \exp (i \omega t), \\ p=\operatorname{Re} \tilde{p}(x) \exp (i \omega t) \\ v=\operatorname{Re} \tilde{v}(x) \exp (i \omega t) \end{array}$$ Thus, to satisfy the boundary condition of $p$, we have $$\tilde{p}(x)=\frac{f_{0}}{A} \cos (k x)+\lambda \sin (k x),$$ where $\lambda$ is to be determined. On the other hand, the macroscope motion of fluid satisfies the Euler equation $$\rho_{0} \frac{\partial v}{\partial t}=-\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$ where $\rho_{0}$ is the average density, $v$ is the velocity and $p$ is the pressure. Then $\tilde{v}(x)=\frac{i k}{\omega \rho_{0}}\left(-p_{0} \sin k x+\lambda \cos k x\right)$, where $p_{0}=\frac{f_{0}}{A}$. Using the boundary condition $v(L)=0$, we have $$\lambda=p_{0} \tan (k L) .$$ Thus $$\begin{array}{c} \tilde{v}(x=0)=\frac{i k}{\omega \rho_{0}} p_{0} \tan k L=\frac{i}{c} \frac{p_{0}}{\rho_{0}} \tan \frac{\omega L}{c} . \\ v(t)=\operatorname{Re}\left(\tilde{v}(0) e^{i \omega t}\right)=-\left(\frac{p_{0}}{c \rho_{0}} \tan \frac{\omega L}{c}\right) \sin \omega t . \end{array}$$

raw_question_text: A U-tube with arms of different cross‐sectional areas $A_{1}, A_{2}$ is filled with an incompressible liquid to a height $d$, as shown. Air is blown impulsively into one end of the tube. Describe quantitatively the subsequent motion of the liquid. You may neglect surface tension effects and the viscosity of the fluid.
normalized_question_text: A U-tube with arms of different cross-sectional areas $A_{1}, A_{2}$ is filled with an incompressible liquid to a height $d$, as shown. Air is blown impulsively into one end of the tube. Describe quantitatively the subsequent motion of the liquid. You may neglect surface tension effects and the viscosity of the fluid.
reasoning_chain: The motion of the fluid is irrotational and non‐steady, and is described by Bernoulli's equation of the form $\frac{1}{2}\rho v^{2}+p+U-\rho\frac{\partial\phi}{\partial t}=\text{constant}$ which holds for all points of the fluid at any given time. Consider two surface points on each arm of the vessel at distances $x_{1}, x_{2}$ from the equilibrium level $d$. Bernoulli's equation gives $\frac{1}{2}\rho v_{1}^{2}+p_{1}+U_{1}-\rho\frac{\partial\phi_{1}}{\partial t}=\frac{1}{2}\rho v_{2}^{2}+p_{2}+U_{2}-\rho\frac{\partial\phi_{2}}{\partial t}$, with $p_{1}=p_{2}=\text{atmospheric pressure}$, $U_{1}=(d+x_{1})\rho g$, $U_{2}=(d-x_{2})\rho g$, $v_{1}=\dot{x}_{1}$, $v_{2}=\dot{x}_{2}$, and approximations $\frac{\partial\phi_{1}}{\partial t}\approx-\ddot{x}_{1}d$, $\frac{\partial\phi_{2}}{\partial t}\approx\ddot{x}_{2}d$. Retaining only first order terms, Bernoulli's equation becomes $(\ddot{x}_{1}+\ddot{x}_{2})+\frac{g}{d}(x_{1}+x_{2})=0$. Making use of the continuity equation $A_{1}x_{1}=A_{2}x_{2}$, we obtain $\ddot{x}_{1}+\frac{gx_{1}}{d}=0$ and $\ddot{x}_{2}+\frac{gx_{2}}{d}=0$. Hence the subsequent motion of the liquid is that of harmonic vibration with angular frequency $\omega=\sqrt{\frac{g}{d}}$.

raw_question_text: The one-dimensional quantum mechanical potential energy of a particle of mass $m$ is given by $$ V(x)=\begin{cases} V_{0}\delta(x), & -a<x<\infty \\ \infty, & x<-a \end{cases} $$ as shown in Fig. 1.19. At time $t=0$, the wave function of the particle is completely confined to the region $-a<x<0$. [Define the quantities $k=\sqrt{2 m E}/\hbar$ and $\alpha=2 m V_{0}/\hbar^{2}$] Give an expression for the time development of the wave function in terms of $f(k)$. What values of $k$ are expected to govern the time behavior at large times?
normalized_question_text: The one-dimensional quantum mechanical potential energy of a particle of mass $m$ is given by $$V(x)=\begin{cases}V_{0}\delta(x), & -a<x<\infty \\ \infty, & x<-a\end{cases}$$ At time $t=0$, the wave function of the particle is completely confined to the region $-a<x<0$. Define $k=\sqrt{2mE}/\hbar$ and $\alpha=2mV_{0}/\hbar^{2}$. Give an expression for the time development of the wave function in terms of $f(k)$. What values of $k$ are expected to govern the time behavior at large times?
raw_answer_text: $\psi(x,t)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(k)\psi_{k}(x)e^{-iE_{k}t/\hbar}dk,\quad \text{with small } k \text{ dominating at large } t$
normalized_answer_text: $\psi(x,t)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(k)\psi_k(x)e^{-iE_k t/\hbar}\,dk,\quad \text{with small } k \text{ dominating at large } t$
reasoning_chain: (e) Since $$ \psi(x,0)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(k)\psi_{k}(x)dk, $$ the time development is given by $$ \psi(x,t)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(k)\psi_{k}(x)e^{-iE_{k}t/\hbar}dk, \quad E_{k}=\frac{\hbar^2k^2}{2m}. $$ For large times, the rapidly oscillating factor $e^{-iE_{k}t/\hbar}$ suppresses contributions from larger $k$, so that the small $k$ components dominate the behavior.

raw_question_text: Now ignore the drag force. Assume that in addition to the Newtonian gravitational potential, the planet experiences a small additional potential so that its potential energy is actually $V(r)=-\frac{G M m}{r}+\frac{\varepsilon}{r^{2}}$, where $\varepsilon$ is a small constant. Calculate the rate of precession of the planetary perihelion, to lowest order in $\varepsilon$. You may assume the orbit is almost circular. In other words, you are to calculate the angle $\varphi$ sketched in Fig. 1.47.
normalized_question_text: Ignore the drag force. In addition to the Newtonian gravitational potential, assume the planet experiences a small extra potential, so its potential energy is $V(r)=-\frac{G M m}{r}+\frac{\varepsilon}{r^{2}}$, where $\varepsilon$ is a small constant. Calculate the rate of precession of the planetary perihelion to lowest order in $\varepsilon$. You may assume the orbit is almost circular. That is, calculate the angle $\varphi$ sketched in Fig. 1.47.
reasoning_chain: The planet is now moving in a central potential $V(r)=-\frac{G M m}{r}+\frac{\varepsilon}{r^{2}}$, and its total energy is $E=\frac{1}{2}m\dot{r}^{2}+\frac{J^{2}}{2mr^{2}}+V(r)$ with $J=m r^{2}\dot{\varphi}$. Writing $\dot{r}=\dot{\varphi}\frac{dr}{d\varphi}$ gives $dr=\frac{r^{2}}{J}\sqrt{2m(E-V)-\frac{J^{2}}{r^{2}}}d\varphi$. In the unperturbed field $V_{0}=-\frac{G M m}{r}$ the orbit is an ellipse with total angular advance $2\pi$. The addition of a small perturbation $\delta V=\frac{\varepsilon}{r^{2}}$ and expanding the integrals in a Taylor series yields a first-order correction computed as $\varphi=\frac{\partial}{\partial J}\left[\frac{2m}{J}\int_{0}^{\pi}r^{2}\delta V\,d\varphi\right]$. With $\delta V=\frac{\varepsilon}{r^{2}}$, the integration leads to $\varphi=\frac{\partial}{\partial J}\left[\frac{2m\varepsilon\pi}{J}\right]$, which gives $\varphi=-\frac{2\pi\varepsilon m}{J^{2}}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{S_{2}} + \frac{1}{S_{2}'} &= \frac{1}{f_{2}}, \\
\frac{1}{S_{3}} + \frac{1}{S_{3}'} &= \frac{1}{f_{3}},
\end{aligned}
$$

where $S_{2} = 10\,\mathrm{cm}$ and $f_{2} = f_{3} = 2\,\mathrm{cm}$.

Solving the two equations in turn, we have
$$
S_{2}' = 2.5\,\mathrm{cm}, \quad S_{3} = 2\,\mathrm{cm} - 2.5\,\mathrm{cm} = -0.5\,\mathrm{cm},
$$
and hence
$$
S_{3}' = 0.4\,\mathrm{cm},
$$
i.e., the exit pupil is located $0.4\,\mathrm{cm}$ behind $L_{3}$. 

From the similar triangles, we have $\frac{D'}{D} = \frac{f_{2}}{f_{1}}$, so that the diameter of the exit pupil is
$$
D' = \frac{f_{3}}{f_{1}} D = 0.8\,\mathrm{cm}.
$$

raw_question_text: In Fig. 2.68 a block of semiconductor (conductivity $=\sigma$ ) has its bottom face $(z=0)$ attached to a metal plate (its conductivity $\sigma\rightarrow\infty$) which is held at potential $\phi=0$. A wire carrying current $J$ is attached to the center of the top face $(z=c)$. The sides $(x=0, x=a, y=0, y=b)$ are insulated and the top is insulated except for the wire. Assume that the charge density is $\rho=0$ and $\varepsilon=\mu=1$ inside the block. (b) Write down the boundary conditions for all faces and express the arbitrary constants in the solution from (a) in terms of the given quantities.
normalized_question_text: In Fig. 2.68 a block of semiconductor (conductivity $=\sigma$) has its bottom face $(z=0)$ attached to a metal plate (its conductivity $\sigma\rightarrow\infty$) which is held at potential $\phi=0$. A wire carrying current $J$ is attached to the center of the top face $(z=c)$. The sides $(x=0, x=a, y=0, y=b)$ are insulated and the top is insulated except for the wire. Assume that the charge density is $\rho=0$ and $\varepsilon=\mu=1$ inside the block. (b) Write down the boundary conditions for all faces and express the arbitrary constants in the solution from (a) in terms of the given quantities.
reasoning_chain: The boundary condition $\mathbf{E}_t=0$ on the vertical surfaces gives $\left.\frac{\partial\phi}{\partial x}\right|_{x=0,a}=0$ and $\left.\frac{\partial\phi}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0,b}=0$. For the top face ($z=c$), Ohm's law gives $\left.\frac{\partial\phi}{\partial z}\right|_{z=c}=-E_z=-\frac{J}{\sigma}\delta\left(x-\frac{a}{2},y-\frac{b}{2}\right)$, while the bottom face ($z=0$) has $\phi(x,y,0)=0$. These conditions require $B=D=0$, $\alpha=\frac{m\pi}{a}$, $\beta=\frac{n\pi}{b}$, and $\gamma=\pi\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2+\left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2}$; additionally, from the symmetry and the condition at $z=0$, we get $F=-E$. Thus, for a given set of integers $m,n$, we have $\phi_{mn}(x,y,z)=A_{mn}\cos\left(\frac{m\pi}{a}x\right)\cos\left(\frac{n\pi}{b}y\right)\sinh(\gamma_{mn}z)$ and the general solution is $\phi(x,y,z)=\sum_{m,n=1}^{\infty}A_{mn}\cos\left(\frac{m\pi}{a}x\right)\cos\left(\frac{n\pi}{b}y\right)\sinh(\gamma_{mn}z)$. Substituting into the top face condition leads to $\sum_{m,n=1}^{\infty}A_{mn}\cos\left(\frac{m\pi}{a}x\right)\cos\left(\frac{n\pi}{b}y\right)\gamma_{mn}\cosh(\gamma_{mn}c)=\frac{J}{\sigma}\delta\left(x-\frac{a}{2},y-\frac{b}{2}\right)$. Multiplying by $\cos\left(\frac{m\pi}{a}x\right)\cos\left(\frac{n\pi}{b}y\right)$ and integrating over the top surface, one obtains $A_{mn}=\frac{4J}{ab\sigma\gamma_{mn}\cosh(\gamma_{mn}c)}\cos\frac{m\pi}{2}\cos\frac{n\pi}{2}$. Note that $A_{mn}\neq0$ only for $m$ and $n$ both even.

raw_question_text: A uniform thin rigid rod of mass $M$ is supported by two rapidly rotating rollers, whose axes are separated by a fixed distance $a$. The rod is initially placed at rest asymmetrically, as shown in Fig. 1.114. Assume that the rollers rotate in opposite directions as shown in the figure. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the bar and the rollers is $\mu$. Write down the equation of motion of the bar and solve for the displacement $x(t)$ of the center $C$ of the bar from roller 1 assuming $x(0)=x_{0}$ and $\dot{x}(0)=0$.
normalized_question_text: A uniform thin rigid rod of mass $M$ is supported by two rapidly rotating rollers, whose axes are separated by a fixed distance $a$. The rod is initially placed at rest asymmetrically, as shown in Fig. 1.114. Assume that the rollers rotate in opposite directions as shown in the figure. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the bar and the rollers is $\mu$. Write down the equation of motion of the bar and solve for the displacement $x(t)$ of the center $C$ of the bar from roller 1 assuming $x(0)=x_{0}$ and $\dot{x}(0)=0$.
reasoning_chain: For equilibrium along the vertical direction we require $N_{1}+N_{2}=Mg$, $aN_{2}=xMg$, giving $N_{1}=\left(1-\frac{x}{a}\right)Mg$, $N_{2}=\frac{x}{a}Mg$. The kinetic friction forces are $f_{1}=\mu N_{1}$, $f_{2}=\mu N_{2}$, with directions as shown in the figure. Newton's second law then gives $M\ddot{x}=f_{1}-f_{2}=\frac{\mu Mg}{a}(a-2x)$. With $\xi=2x-a$, the above becomes $\ddot{\xi}=-\frac{2\mu g}{a}\xi$, which is the equation of motion of a harmonic oscillator. With the initial conditions $\xi=2x_{0}-a$, $\dot{\xi}=0$ at $t=0$, the solution is $\xi=\left(2x_{0}-a\right)\cos(\omega t)$, where $\omega=\sqrt{\frac{2\mu g}{a}}$. Hence $x=\left(x_{0}-\frac{a}{2}\right)\cos(\omega t)+\frac{a}{2}$.

raw_question_text: A beam of electrons is scattered by a fixed scattering target as shown in Fig. 3.27. The electrons are elastically scattered. Each electron has an energy $E=\frac{5}{3} m_{0} c^{2}$ and the beam has a flux of $Q$ electrons per second. What are the magnitude and direction of the force on the scattering target due to the electrons?
normalized_question_text: A beam of electrons is scattered by a fixed scattering target, as shown in Fig. 3.27. The electrons are elastically scattered. Each electron has energy $E=\frac{5}{3} m_{0} c^{2}$, and the beam has a flux of $Q$ electrons per second. What are the magnitude and direction of the force on the scattering target due to the electrons?
raw_answer_text: $\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{3} Q m_{0} c$, acting vertically onto the target
normalized_answer_text: $\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{3} Q m_{0} c$, acting vertically onto the target
reasoning_chain: Since the electrons are elastically scattered, they have the same speed before and after scattering and conservation of the momentum parallel to the target requires that the incident and scattering angles are equal. Then after scattering the normal component of the momentum changes sign but remains the same in magnitude. Hence $\Delta p=2 p_{n}=2 m_{0}\,\gamma v \cos 45^{\circ}=\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{3} m_{0} c$. The force $F$ on the target due to the beam of electrons is equal to the impulse given to it per unit time by the beam. As $Q$ electrons impinge on the target in unit time, $F=2 p_{n} Q=\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{3} Q m_{0} c$ and it acts vertically onto the target.

raw_question_text: A simple pendulum consisting of a mass $m$ and a weightless string of length $l$ is mounted on a support of mass $M$ which is attached to a horizontal spring with force constant $k$ as shown in Fig. 2.51. Find the frequencies for small oscillations.
normalized_question_text: A simple pendulum consisting of a mass $m$ and a weightless string of length $l$ is mounted on a support of mass $M$ which is attached to a horizontal spring with force constant $k$ as shown in Fig. 2.51. Find the frequencies for small oscillations.
reasoning_chain:
For small oscillations, $x$, $	heta$, $\dot{x}$, and $\dot{	heta}$ are small quantities. Neglecting terms of orders higher than two, the equations of motion become
\[
 egin{aligned}
(M+m)\,\ddot{x}+ml\,\ddot{	heta}+kx&=0,\
l\,\ddot{	heta}+\ddot{x}+g	heta&=0.
\end{aligned}
\]
Set
\[
x=A\exp(i\omega t),\quad 	heta=B\exp(i\omega t).
\]
These equations become
\[
 egin{pmatrix}
k-(M+m)\omega^{2} & -ml\omega^{2}\
-\omega^{2} & g-l\omega^{2}
\end{pmatrix}
 egin{pmatrix} A\ B \end{pmatrix}=0.
\]
The secular equation
\[
\left| egin{array}{cc}
k-(M+m)\omega^{2} & -ml\omega^{2}\
-\omega^{2} & g-l\omega^{2}
\end{array}ight|=Ml\omega^{2}-[g(M+m)+kl]\omega^{2}+gk=0
\]
has two positive roots
\[
 egin{array}{l}
\omega_{1}=\left[ rac{g(M+m)+kl+\sqrt{[g(M+m)+kl]^{2}-4Mlgk}}{2Ml}ight]^{1/2}, \
\omega_{2}=\left[ rac{g(M+m)+kl-\sqrt{[g(M+m)+kl]^{2}-4Mlgk}}{2Ml}ight]^{1/2},
\end{array}
\]
which are the normal-mode angular frequencies of the system.
